member.name: T:KSP.Sim.impl.CollisionManager
member.summary:
Manage PhysX interactions to ignore collisions between colliders in the same vessel.
The primary mechanism for this is Physics.IgnoreCollision(). However, doing that for every collider combination scales O(n^2). Scalability problems become measurable around 100 parts, and can take several minutes after 900+ parts.
It is much faster to only set up ignores for parts that are actually touching. This scales close to O(n) with the collider count. However, it's difficult to test this in advance. So we just let PhysX tell us which are touching, because that's what it's designed to do.
- Record each collider as being "owned" by a vessel (guid) - Wait for PhysX to report a contact. - CollisionMonitor receives a Physics.ContactModifyEvent for the contact. (The callback is offthread) - If the contact is between to colliders owned by the same vessel GUID, void the contact. (This allows them to pass through as if already ignored) - Log the contact in a thread-safe structure. - At some later point on the main thread, read the log, set do the Physics.IgnoreCollision(), and (optionally) log again so that it can be undone.
There are some cases that require special handling:
- Collider setups that won't generate Physics.ContactModifyEvent() callbacks (usually because they are temporarily on the same Rigidbody), but need to be ignored anyway. It is necessary to call Physics.IgnoreCollision() in advance. ("always ignore") - Some ignores need to persist despite the collider being removed from the vessel. ("persist after removal"). This is usually a separable item (e.g., engine shroud) significantly overlaps other colliders (e.g., the engine it contains). If the ignore is removed too soon, the resulting contact event can lead to RUD.

member.name: M:KSP.Sim.impl.PartComponentModule_SolarPanel.CalculateVesselSolarLOS
member.summary: Calculates the total solar exposure for the vessel by evaluating the line-of-sight and flux from all active stars.
member.returns: A representing the cumulative solar intensity factor (normalized to home-world solar constant).
member.remarks: The method iterates through all stellar bodies tracked by the vessel's . For each star: 1. It checks for occlusion using . 2. If clear, it adds the star's flux (normalized by ) to the total. 3. If blocked, it updates with the name of the obstructing object.

member.name: M:KSP.Sim.impl.PartComponentModule_SolarPanel.CalculateOcclusionPerStar(KSP.Sim.impl.CelestialBodyComponent,System.String@)
member.summary: Determines if a specific star is occluded by the vessel's current primary celestial body (MainBody).
member.param: The stellar body being checked for visibility.
Output parameter containing the display name of the obstructing body if occluded.
member.returns: True if the star is visible; false if it is hidden behind the main body.
member.remarks: This method uses a geometric sphere-occlusion algorithm. It calculates the intersection of the line-of-sight vector with the main body's radius using vector projections and dot products.

member.name: M:KSP.Sim.impl.PartOwnerComponent.DetachPart(KSP.Sim.impl.PartComponent,System.Nullable{System.Guid},System.Boolean,System.Boolean)
member.summary:
Recursively remove everything attached to from the PartOwnerComponent that currently owns it, and move them to a new vessel. Various data from the previous vessel is carried over, such as science data.
If is false and the part is still attached to the vessel, then this has the effect of destroying the original VesselComponent and creating a similar one rooted at .
member.param: The part to remove from the vessel.
GUID of a subvessel record on the part's current vessel to use to construct the new vessel.
If this method should sever all of the current attachments to
If the process of dermining what is connected to should ignore attachments flagged with IsCompoundJoint. (Struts, joint refinorcement, fuel tubes, etc.

member.name: M:KSP.Game.Science.ScienceExperimentsDataStore.GetFlavorText(System.String,System.String,KSP.Game.Science.ScienceReportType)
member.summary: Get the flavor text localization tag for the specified experiment, research location and report type. We first look for flavor texts that match the situation and science region. If we find none, we fall back to flavor texts that only match the situation. If multiple matching flavor texts are found, one is chosen at random.
member.param: ID of the experiment, e.g. CrewObservation.
ID of the research location, e.g. Jool_HighOrbit_JoolEquator.
Type of report, either Data or Sample.
member.returns: Chosen experiment flavor text localization tag.

member.name: M:KSP.Modules.Module_SolarPanel.CalculateTrackingLOS(UnityEngine.Vector3,System.String@)
member.summary: Evaluates line-of-sight (LOS) between the solar panel and its tracking target.
member.param: The normalized vector toward the light source.
Output string identifying the obstructing object (Body, Terrain, or Part).
member.returns: True if the panel is receiving light; false if obstructed.
member.remarks: Logic flow: 1. Checks for simulated orbital occlusion (SimBlockingBody). 2. Validates panel orientation relative to the light source (Incidence Angle). 3. Performs a physical Raycast to detect terrain (PQSRenderer) or vessel self-shadowing (PartBehavior). 4. Updates localization strings for the UI display.

member.summary: Represents a library import

summary.para: The library being imported, can take a few formats
Local library: "name", imports a local (to the patch folder for the mod) file named "_name" or if there is any pathing it only prepends the underscore after the last slash
External library: "guid:name", imports a file from another mod (defined in the guid) named "_name"
Builtin library: "builtin:name", imports a builtin library named "name"

member.name: M:MoonSharp.Interpreter.Interop.BasicDescriptors.DispatchingUserDataDescriptor.MetaIndex(MoonSharp.Interpreter.Script,System.Object,System.String)
member.summary:
Gets a "meta" operation on this userdata. If a descriptor does not support this functionality, it should return "null" (not a nil). See for further details.
If a method exists marked with for the specific metamethod requested, that method is returned.
If the above fails, the following dispatching occur:
__add, __sub, __mul, __div, __mod and __unm are dispatched to C# operator overloads (if they exist) __eq is dispatched to System.Object.Equals. __lt and __le are dispatched IComparable.Compare, if the type implements IComparable or IComparable{object} __len is dispatched to Length and Count properties, if those exist. __iterator is handled if the object implements IEnumerable or IEnumerator. __tonumber is dispatched to implicit or explicit conversion operators to standard numeric types. __tobool is dispatched to an implicit or explicit conversion operator to bool. If that fails, operator true is used.
The script originating the request The object (null if a static request is done) The name of the metamember.

member.name: T:MoonSharp.Interpreter.Interop.LuaStateInterop.LuaBase
M:MoonSharp.Interpreter.Interop.LuaStateInterop.LuaBase.LuaCall(MoonSharp.Interpreter.Interop.LuaStateInterop.LuaState,System.Int32,System.Int32)
member.summary: Calls a function. To call a function you must use the following protocol: first, the function to be called is pushed onto the stack; then, the arguments to the function are pushed in direct order; that is, the first argument is pushed first. Finally you call lua_call; nargs is the number of arguments that you pushed onto the stack. All arguments and the function value are popped from the stack when the function is called. The function results are pushed onto the stack when the function returns. The number of results is adjusted to nresults, unless nresults is LUA_MULTRET. In this case, all results from the function are pushed. Lua takes care that the returned values fit into the stack space. The function results are pushed onto the stack in direct order (the first result is pushed first), so that after the call the last result is on the top of the stack.

member.summary: Gets a "meta" operation on this userdata. If a descriptor does not support this functionality, it should return "null" (not a nil). These standard metamethods can be supported (the return value should be a function accepting the classic parameters of the corresponding metamethod): __add, __sub, __mul, __div, __div, __pow, __unm, __eq, __lt, __le, __lt, __len, __concat, __pairs, __ipairs, __iterator, __call These standard metamethods are supported through other calls for efficiency: __index, __newindex, __tostring
member.param: The object (null if a static request is done)

member.name: T:MoonSharp.Interpreter.Interop.IGeneratorUserDataDescriptor
member.summary: An interface for type descriptors having the ability to generate other descriptors on the fly.

member.name: M:MoonSharp.Interpreter.Interop.IGeneratorUserDataDescriptor.Generate(System.Type)
member.summary:
Generates a new descriptor for the specified type.
The purpose is to allow a mechanism by which a type descriptor can replace itself with another descriptor for a specific type. For example, descriptors can be created on the fly to support generic types through this mechanism.
The return value should be: null - if this descriptor is simply skipped for the specified type this - acts as if the descriptor was a vanilla descriptor a new descriptor - if a new descriptor should be used in place of this one
It's recommended that instances of descriptors are cached for future references. One possible way, to do the caching is to have the generator register the descriptor through . In that case, it should query whether the type is exactly registered, through
NOTE-1 : the search for descriptors does NOT stop with the descriptor returned by this type, but other descriptors (e.g. for interfaces) might still be added.
NOTE-2 : the descriptor generation mechanism is not triggered on an exact match of types.
NOTE-3 : the method is called in the context of a lock over the descriptors registry so no unpredictable changes to the registry can come from other threads during the execution of this method. However this method should not take other locks, to avoid deadlocks.
member.returns: Null, this object instance or a new descriptor.

member.name: T:MoonSharp.Interpreter.Interop.InteropRegistrationPolicy
member.summary: Collection of the standard policies to handle UserData type registrations. Provided mostly for compile-time backward compatibility with old code. See also : .

member.name: P:MoonSharp.Interpreter.Interop.InteropRegistrationPolicy.Default
member.summary:
The default registration policy used by MoonSharp unless explicitely replaced. Deregistrations are allowed, but registration of a new descriptor are not allowed if a descriptor is already registered for that type.
Types must be explicitly registered.

member.name: P:MoonSharp.Interpreter.Interop.InteropRegistrationPolicy.Explicit
member.summary:
The default registration policy used by MoonSharp unless explicitely replaced. Deregistrations are allowed, but registration of a new descriptor are not allowed if a descriptor is already registered for that type.
Types must be explicitly registered.

member.name: P:MoonSharp.Interpreter.Interop.InteropRegistrationPolicy.Automatic
member.summary: Types are automatically registered if not found in the registry. This is easier to use but potentially unsafe.

member.summary: Converts this userdata to string

member.name: M:MoonSharp.Interpreter.Interop.ProxyUserDataDescriptor.MetaIndex(MoonSharp.Interpreter.Script,System.Object,System.String)
member.summary: Gets a "meta" operation on this userdata. If a descriptor does not support this functionality, it should return "null" (not a nil). These standard metamethods can be supported (the return value should be a function accepting the classic parameters of the corresponding metamethod): __add, __sub, __mul, __div, __div, __pow, __unm, __eq, __lt, __le, __lt, __len, __concat, __pairs, __ipairs, __iterator, __call These standard metamethods are supported through other calls for efficiency: __index, __newindex, __tostring
member.param: The object (null if a static request is done)

member.summary: Determines whether the specified object is compatible with the specified type. Unless a very specific behaviour is needed, the correct implementation is a simple " return type.IsInstanceOfType(obj); "

member.summary: Checks if the event is compatible with a standard descriptor
member.param: if set to true an exception with the proper error message is thrown if not compatible.
member.exception: Thrown if throwException is true and one of this applies: The event is declared in a value type or The event does not have both add and remove methods or The event handler type doesn't implement a public Invoke method or The event handler has a return type which is not System.Void or The event handler has more than MAX_ARGS_IN_DELEGATE parameters or The event handler has a value type parameter or a by ref parameter or The event handler signature is not a valid method according to

member.name: T:MoonSharp.Interpreter.FuzzySymbolMatchingBehavior
member.summary: A flag that controls if/how symbols (method, property, userdata) are fuzzily matched when they do not exist. Flags can be combined for multiple checks.

member.name: F:MoonSharp.Interpreter.FuzzySymbolMatchingBehavior.None
member.summary: No fuzzy matching is performed.

member.name: F:MoonSharp.Interpreter.FuzzySymbolMatchingBehavior.UpperFirstLetter
member.summary: The first letter of a symbol will be uppercased (to check for common C# naming conventions). For example, testMethod() becomes TestMethod()

member.name: F:MoonSharp.Interpreter.FuzzySymbolMatchingBehavior.Camelify
member.summary: Underscores in symbols are converted to camelcase. For example, test_method() becomes testMethod()

member.name: F:MoonSharp.Interpreter.FuzzySymbolMatchingBehavior.PascalCase
member.summary: Converts a symbol to pascal case. For example, test_Method_two() becomes TestMethodTwo()

member.name: T:MoonSharp.Interpreter.ScriptOptions
member.summary: This class contains options to customize behaviour of Script objects.

member.name: P:MoonSharp.Interpreter.ScriptOptions.ScriptLoader
member.summary: Gets or sets the current script-loader.

member.name: P:MoonSharp.Interpreter.ScriptOptions.DebugPrint
member.summary: Gets or sets the debug print handler

member.name: P:MoonSharp.Interpreter.ScriptOptions.DebugInput
member.summary: Gets or sets the debug input handler (takes a prompt as an input, for interactive interpreters, like debug.debug).

member.name: P:MoonSharp.Interpreter.ScriptOptions.UseLuaErrorLocations
member.summary: Gets or sets a value indicating whether error messages will use Lua error locations instead of MoonSharp improved ones. Use this for compatibility with legacy Lua code which parses error messages.

member.name: P:MoonSharp.Interpreter.ScriptOptions.ColonOperatorClrCallbackBehaviour
member.summary: Gets or sets a value which dictates the behaviour of the colon (':') operator in callbacks to CLR code.

member.name: P:MoonSharp.Interpreter.ScriptOptions.Stdin
member.summary: Gets or sets the stream used as stdin. If null, a default stream is used.

member.name: P:MoonSharp.Interpreter.ScriptOptions.Stdout
member.summary: Gets or sets the stream used as stdout. If null, a default stream is used.

member.name: P:MoonSharp.Interpreter.ScriptOptions.Stderr
member.summary: Gets or sets the stream used as stderr. If null, a default stream is used.

member.name: P:MoonSharp.Interpreter.ScriptOptions.TailCallOptimizationThreshold
member.summary: Gets or sets the stack depth threshold at which MoonSharp starts doing tail call optimizations. TCOs can provide the little benefit of avoiding stack overflows in corner case scenarios, at the expense of losing debug information and error stack traces in all other, more common scenarios. MoonSharp choice is to start performing TCOs only after a certain threshold of stack usage is reached - by default half the current stack depth (128K entries), thus 64K entries, on either the internal stacks. Set this to int.MaxValue to disable TCOs entirely, or to 0 to always have TCOs enabled.

member.name: P:MoonSharp.Interpreter.ScriptOptions.CheckThreadAccess
member.summary:
Gets or sets a value indicating whether the thread check is enabled. A "lazy" thread check is performed everytime execution is entered to ensure that no two threads calls MoonSharp execution concurrently. However 1) the check is performed best effort (thus, it might not detect all issues) and 2) it might trigger in very odd legal situations (like, switching threads inside a CLR-callback without actually having concurrency.
Disable this option if the thread check is giving problems in your scenario, but please check that you are not calling MoonSharp execution concurrently as it is not supported.

member.name: T:MoonSharp.Interpreter.Platforms.IPlatformAccessor
member.summary:
Interface to abstract all accesses made to the underlying platform (OS, framework) by the scripting engine. Can be used both to support "non-standard" platforms (i.e. non-posix, non-windows) and/or to sandbox the behaviour of the scripting engine.
It's recommended that no class implement IPlatformAccessor directly, and rather extend .

member.name: T:MoonSharp.Interpreter.Loaders.IScriptLoader
member.summary:
Class dictating how requests to read scripts from files are handled.
It's recommended that no class implement IScriptLoader directly, and rather extend ScriptLoaderBase.

member.name: M:MoonSharp.Interpreter.Loaders.IScriptLoader.LoadFile(System.String,MoonSharp.Interpreter.Table)
member.summary: Opens a file for reading the script code. It can return either a string, a byte[] or a Stream. If a byte[] is returned, the content is assumed to be a serialized (dumped) bytecode. If it's a string, it's assumed to be either a script or the output of a string.dump call. If a Stream, autodetection takes place.
member.returns: A string, a byte[] or a Stream.

The resolution happens first on paths included in the LUA_PATH global variable (if and only if the IgnoreLuaPathGlobal is false), and - if the variable does not exist - by consulting the ScriptOptions.ModulesPaths array. Override to provide a different behaviour.

member.name: T:MoonSharp.Interpreter.Loaders.UnityAssetsScriptLoader
member.summary:
A script loader which can load scripts from assets in Unity3D. Scripts should be saved as .txt files in a subdirectory of Assets/Resources.
When MoonSharp is activated on Unity3D and the default script loader is used, scripts should be saved as .txt files in Assets/Resources/MoonSharp/Scripts.

member.param: The path, relative to Assets/Resources. For example if your scripts are stored under Assets/Resources/Scripts, you should pass the value "Scripts". If null, "MoonSharp/Scripts" is used.

If it's a string, it's assumed to be either a script or the output of a string.dump call. If a Stream, autodetection takes place.
member.returns: A string, a byte[] or a Stream.

member.name: T:MoonSharp.Interpreter.REPL.ReplInterpreterScriptLoader
member.summary:
A script loader loading scripts directly from the file system (does not go through platform object) AND starts with module paths taken from environment variables (again, not going through the platform object).
The paths are preconstructed using : * The MOONSHARP_PATH environment variable if it exists * The LUA_PATH_5_2 environment variable if MOONSHARP_PATH does not exists * The LUA_PATH environment variable if LUA_PATH_5_2 and MOONSHARP_PATH do not exists * The "?;?.lua" path if all the above fail
Also, everytime a module is require(d), the "LUA_PATH" global variable is checked. If it exists, those paths will be used to load the module instead of the global ones.

Override to provide a different behaviour.

member.summary: Class performing conversions between Tables and Json. NOTE : the conversions are done respecting json syntax but using Lua constructs. This means mostly that: 1) Lua string escapes can be accepted while they are not technically valid JSON, and viceversa 2) Null values are represented using a static userdata of type JsonNull 3) Do not use it when input cannot be entirely trusted

member.name: P:MoonSharp.Interpreter.Script.Registry
member.summary: MoonSharp (like Lua itself) provides a registry, a predefined table that can be used by any CLR code to store whatever Lua values it needs to store. Any CLR code can store data into this table, but it should take care to choose keys that are different from those used by other libraries, to avoid collisions. Typically, you should use as key a string GUID, a string containing your library name, or a userdata with the address of a CLR object in your code.

member.name: M:MoonSharp.Interpreter.AutoDescribingUserDataDescriptor.MetaIndex(MoonSharp.Interpreter.Script,System.Object,System.String)
member.summary: Gets a "meta" operation on this userdata. If a descriptor does not support this functionality, it should return "null" (not a nil). These standard metamethods can be supported (the return value should be a function accepting the classic parameters of the corresponding metamethod): __add, __sub, __mul, __div, __div, __pow, __unm, __eq, __lt, __le, __lt, __len, __concat, __pairs, __ipairs, __iterator, __call These standard metamethods are supported through other calls for efficiency: __index, __newindex, __tostring
member.param: The object (null if a static request is done)

member.name: F:MoonSharp.Interpreter.TypeValidationFlags.AutoConvert
member.summary: Simple autoconversions are attempted: 1) Numbers are convertible to strings 2) Strings are convertible to numbers if they contain a number 3) Everything is convertible to boolean (with void and nil converting to 'false', everything else converting to 'true') Note: if both AutoConvert and AllowNil are specified, nils will NOT be converted to false booleans.

member.name: T:MoonSharp.Interpreter.RefIdObject
member.summary: A base class for many MoonSharp objects. Helds a ReferenceID property which gets a different value for every object instance, for debugging purposes. Note that the ID is not assigned in a thread safe manner for speed reason, so the IDs are guaranteed to be unique only if everything is running on one thread at a time.

member.summary: Gets this coroutine as a typed enumerable which can be looped over for resuming. Returns its result as the specified type. Only the first element of tuples is returned. Only non-CLR coroutines can be resumed with this method. Use an overload of the Resume method accepting a ScriptExecutionContext instead.
member.exception: Only non-CLR coroutines can be resumed with this overload of the Resume method. Use the overload accepting a ScriptExecutionContext instead

member.name: M:MoonSharp.Interpreter.Coroutine.AsUnityCoroutine
member.summary: The purpose of this method is to convert a MoonSharp/Lua coroutine to a Unity3D coroutine. This loops over the coroutine, discarding returned values, and returning null for each invocation. This means however that the coroutine will be invoked each frame. Only non-CLR coroutines can be resumed with this method. Use an overload of the Resume method accepting a ScriptExecutionContext instead.
member.exception: Only non-CLR coroutines can be resumed with this overload of the Resume method. Use the overload accepting a ScriptExecutionContext instead

member.name: T:MoonSharp.Interpreter.MoonSharpModuleMethodAttribute
member.summary:
In a module type, mark methods or fields with this attribute to have them exposed as module functions. Methods must have the signature "public static DynValue ...(ScriptExecutionContextCallbackArguments)". Fields must be static or const strings, with an anonymous Lua function inside.
See for more information about modules.

member.name: T:MoonSharp.Interpreter.InteropAccessMode
member.summary: Enumerations of the possible strategies to marshal CLR objects to MoonSharp userdata and functions when using automatic descriptors. Note that these are "hints" and MoonSharp is free to ignore the access mode specified (if different from HideMembers) and downgrade the access mode to "Reflection". This particularly happens when running on AOT platforms like iOS. See also : and .

member.name: F:MoonSharp.Interpreter.InteropAccessMode.Reflection
member.summary: Optimization is not performed and reflection is used everytime to access members. This is the slowest approach but saves a lot of memory if members are seldomly used.

member.name: F:MoonSharp.Interpreter.InteropAccessMode.LazyOptimized
member.summary: Optimization is done on the fly the first time a member is accessed. This saves memory for all members that are never accessed, at the cost of an increased script execution time.

member.name: F:MoonSharp.Interpreter.InteropAccessMode.Preoptimized
member.summary: Optimization is done at registration time.

member.name: F:MoonSharp.Interpreter.InteropAccessMode.BackgroundOptimized
member.summary: Optimization is done in a background thread which starts at registration time. If a member is accessed before optimization is completed, reflection is used.

member.name: F:MoonSharp.Interpreter.InteropAccessMode.Hardwired
member.summary: Use the hardwired descriptor(s)

member.name: F:MoonSharp.Interpreter.InteropAccessMode.HideMembers
member.summary: No optimization is done, and members are not accessible at all.

member.name: F:MoonSharp.Interpreter.InteropAccessMode.NoReflectionAllowed
member.summary: No reflection is allowed, nor code generation. This is used as a safeguard when registering types which should not use a standard reflection based descriptor - for example for types implementing

member.name: F:MoonSharp.Interpreter.InteropAccessMode.Default
member.summary: Use the default access mode

member.name: T:MoonSharp.Interpreter.MoonSharpModuleAttribute
member.summary:
Marks a CLR type to be a MoonSharp module. Modules are the fastest way to bring interop between scripts and CLR code, albeit at the cost of a very increased complexity in writing them. Modules is what's used for the standard library, for maximum efficiency.
Modules are basically classes containing only static methods, with the callback function signature.
See and for (extension) methods used to register modules to a table.
See for information regarding the standard callback signature along with easier ways to marshal methods.
See for easier object marshalling.

member.name: P:MoonSharp.Interpreter.MoonSharpModuleAttribute.Namespace
member.summary: Gets or sets the namespace, that is the name of the table which will contain the defined functions. Can be null to be in the global table.

member.name: M:MoonSharp.Interpreter.DynValue.NewTailCallReq(MoonSharp.Interpreter.DynValue,MoonSharp.Interpreter.DynValue[])
member.summary: Creates a new request for a tail call. This is the preferred way to execute Lua/MoonSharp code from a callback, although it's not always possible to use it. When a function (callback or script closure) returns a TailCallRequest, the bytecode processor immediately executes the function contained in the request. By executing script in this way, a callback function ensures it's not on the stack anymore and thus a number of functionality (state savings, coroutines, etc) keeps working at full power.

member.name: M:MoonSharp.Interpreter.DynValue.NewTailCallReq(MoonSharp.Interpreter.TailCallData)
member.summary: Creates a new request for a tail call. This is the preferred way to execute Lua/MoonSharp code from a callback, although it's not always possible to use it. When a function (callback or script closure) returns a TailCallRequest, the bytecode processor immediately executes the function contained in the request. By executing script in this way, a callback function ensures it's not on the stack anymore and thus a number of functionality (state savings, coroutines, etc) keeps working at full power.
member.param: The data for the tail call.

member.summary: Class implementing a debugger allowing attaching from a Visual Studio Code debugging session.

member.name: M:MoonSharp.VsCodeDebugger.MoonSharpVsCodeDebugServer.#ctor(System.Int32)
member.summary: Initializes a new instance of the class.
member.param: The port on which the debugger listens. It's recommended to use 41912.

member.name: M:MoonSharp.VsCodeDebugger.MoonSharpVsCodeDebugServer.#ctor(MoonSharp.Interpreter.Script,System.Int32,System.Func{MoonSharp.Interpreter.Debugging.SourceCode,System.String})
member.summary: Initializes a new instance of the class with a default script. Note that for this specific script, it will NOT attach the debugger to the script.
member.param: The port on which the debugger listens. It's recommended to use 41912 unless you are going to keep more than one script object around.

A function which gets in input a source code and returns the path to source file to use. It can return null and in that case (or if the file cannot be found) a temporary file will be generated on the fly.

member.name: M:MoonSharp.VsCodeDebugger.MoonSharpVsCodeDebugServer.AttachToScript(MoonSharp.Interpreter.Script,System.String,System.Func{MoonSharp.Interpreter.Debugging.SourceCode,System.String})
member.summary: Attaches the specified script to the debugger
member.param: A function which gets in input a source code and returns the path to source file to use. It can return null and in that case (or if the file cannot be found) a temporary file will be generated on the fly.
member.exception: If the script has already been attached to this debugger.

member.summary: Detaches the specified script. The debugger attached to that script will get disconnected.

member.name: T:Redux.Fixes.DresRingsFix
member.summary: The rings are displayed behind Dres because of some depth test issues between the local space planet shader and the scaled space rings. This fixes the issue by changing the local-scaled transition to be below the inner radius of the rings (250km to be low enough below the ring's inner radius is 414km).

member.name: T:Redux.CollisionMonitor
member.summary:
Listen for physics contacts from Physics.ContactModifyEvent(), void contacts between the same vessel, and report them to the owning CollisionManager when that happens.
This process is set up as a singleton separate from CollisionManager in order to avoid multiple CollisionManagers (per vessel) getting callbacks for colliders they don't own.
